title: Drug Interactions — NAPLEX High-Yield
description: Clinically significant drug-drug and drug-food interactions: mechanisms, outcomes, and management strategies for the NAPLEX exam.
subject: pharmacy

cards.front: Warfarin + NSAIDs: mechanism and clinical risk
cards.back: NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation (COX-1) AND can displace warfarin from albumin binding sites → ↑ free warfarin → ↑ INR. Combined effect dramatically ↑ bleeding risk. Avoid; use acetaminophen for analgesia if possible

Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 (major warfarin metabolism) and CYP3A4 → ↑ warfarin levels → ↑ INR and bleeding risk. Effect lasts weeks to months after stopping amiodarone (long t½ ~40–55 days). Reduce warfarin dose by ~30–50%
Rifampin is a potent CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inducer → ↑ warfarin metabolism → ↓ INR → risk of thromboembolism. May need to double warfarin dose. INR must be monitored closely

cards.front: Statins + CYP3A4 inhibitors: drugs and risk
cards.back: CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolide antibiotics, HIV protease inhibitors, amiodarone, diltiazem, verapamil) ↑ statin plasma levels → ↑ myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk. Most affected: lovastatin, simvastatin, atorvastatin. Avoid or use alternative statin (pravastatin, rosuvastatin)

Gemfibrozil inhibits simvastatin glucuronidation (CYP2C8/OATP1B1) → ↑ simvastatin acid concentrations → myopathy risk. Combination should generally be avoided. If fibrate needed, fenofibrate is preferred with statins

Both are CYP2C19 substrates. Omeprazole competes for CYP2C19 → ↓ clopidogrel activation → ↓ antiplatelet effect → ↑ cardiovascular event risk. Use pantoprazole (weaker CYP2C19 inhibitor) as alternative PPI

cards.front: MAOIs + tyramine-rich foods: mechanism and outcome
cards.back: MAOIs prevent tyramine degradation → ↑ tyramine absorption → displacement of NE from vesicles → ↑↑ NE release → hypertensive crisis (severe headache, palpitations, stroke risk). Foods: aged cheese, cured meats, red wine, soy products, fava beans

Combined serotonergic activity → serotonin syndrome (fever, clonus, agitation, diarrhea, diaphoresis). Washout: stop MAOI 14 days before SSRI; stop SSRIs 14 days before MAOI (5 weeks for fluoxetine — long t½)
Both ↑ serotonergic tone → potential serotonin syndrome. FDA warning exists but evidence is limited; clinical judgment required. Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms

All reduce renal Na+ and water → compensatory ↑ Li+ reabsorption (follows Na+ in proximal tubule) → lithium toxicity. NSAIDs: inhibit prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow. ACE inhibitors: ↓ GFR. Thiazides: ↑ proximal reabsorption

All ↑ digoxin levels: amiodarone inhibits P-gp and renal clearance; verapamil inhibits P-gp; quinidine inhibits P-gp + renal clearance. Reduce digoxin dose by 50% when adding these agents. Monitor digoxin levels

Chelation: divalent/trivalent cations bind fluoroquinolone → ↓ absorption (up to 90%). Space by 2 hrs before or 6 hrs after. Same applies to tetracyclines. Significance: therapeutic failure, bacterial resistance
↓ Levothyroxine absorption: calcium carbonate, ferrous sulfate, PPIs (↑ gastric pH), cholestyramine/colestipol. Separate by ≥ 4 hours. Monitor TSH after starting or stopping interacting drugs
NSAIDs ↓ renal prostaglandin synthesis → ↓ GFR → ↓ methotrexate renal excretion → methotrexate toxicity (mucositis, myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity). Especially dangerous at higher MTX doses

IV contrast can cause AKI → metformin accumulates → lactic acidosis risk. Hold metformin at time of contrast and 48 hrs after; restart when SCr confirms stable renal function. Especially important if eGFR 30–60
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 → ↓ theophylline metabolism → theophylline toxicity (seizures, arrhythmias, nausea). Monitor theophylline levels and reduce dose. Levofloxacin is safer alternative

Additive hyperkalemia risk. ACE inhibitor alone can ↑ K+; adding spironolactone or ARB significantly ↑ risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia. Triple therapy (ACEI + ARB + aldosterone antagonist) is contraindicated

cards.front: Serotonin syndrome vs anticholinergic toxicity: differentiation
cards.back: Serotonin syndrome: hyperreflexia, clonus, diarrhea, diaphoresis (wet skin). Anticholinergic: urinary retention, constipation, dry skin, absent bowel sounds, mydriasis, tachycardia. Treatment: serotonin → cyproheptadine; anticholinergic → physostigmine

Nitrates → ↑ cGMP → vasodilation. PDE-5 inhibitors prevent cGMP breakdown → further ↑ cGMP → additive vasodilation → profound hypotension (may be fatal). Timeframe: avoid nitrates for 24h after sildenafil/vardenafil; 48h after tadalafil

cards.front: St. John's Wort drug interactions (mechanism and affected drugs)
cards.back: Potent CYP3A4 and P-gp inducer → ↓ levels of: cyclosporine, tacrolimus, HIV protease inhibitors, warfarin, oral contraceptives, digoxin, statins. Clinically significant transplant rejection and HIV treatment failure reported

Disulfiram-like reaction: inhibition of aldehyde dehydrogenase → accumulation of acetaldehyde → flushing, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, hypotension. Avoid alcohol during treatment and 48h after metronidazole (72h after tinidazole)

Additive QT prolongation → risk of torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT). Avoid combining with: antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol), antipsychotics (haloperidol, quetiapine), fluoroquinolones. Monitor ECG

CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, St. John's Wort) ↑ OC metabolism → ↓ ethinyl estradiol levels → contraceptive failure. Use backup method; consider alternative contraception
Additive potassium retention → hyperkalemia. Combination used in HF (spironolactone + ACE inhibitor) but requires close K+ monitoring: check in 1–2 weeks after initiation, then periodically

cards.front: Cyclosporine + CYP3A4 inhibitors vs inducers: examples
cards.back: Inhibitors (↑ cyclosporine, risk of nephrotoxicity/immunosuppression): azole antifungals, clarithromycin, diltiazem, verapamil, grapefruit juice. Inducers (↓ cyclosporine, risk of rejection): rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St. John's Wort

cards.front: Grapefruit juice interactions: mechanism and drugs affected
cards.back: Furanocoumarins in grapefruit inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 → ↑ oral bioavailability of: statins (lovastatin, simvastatin, atorvastatin), CCBs (felodipine, nifedipine, amlodipine), cyclosporine, buspirone, sildenafil. Single glass can last 24+ hrs

Calcium, magnesium, aluminum, iron chelate tetracyclines in GI tract → ↓ absorption by 50–80%. Separate by 2 hrs before or after dairy/antacids/iron. Doxycycline less affected than older tetracyclines

If clonidine stopped abruptly while on β-blocker: withdrawal of central α2 agonism → ↑ sympathetic tone; β-blocker prevents compensatory ↑ HR but leaves peripheral vasoconstriction unopposed → hypertensive crisis. Always taper clonidine

cards.front: Isoniazid (INH) drug interactions: mechanism and drugs
cards.back: INH inhibits CYP2C19 and CYP2E1: ↑ phenytoin levels (toxicity: nystagmus, ataxia), ↑ carbamazepine, ↑ warfarin. INH also causes pyridoxine (B6) deficiency → peripheral neuropathy (supplement B6 with INH)

P-gp is an efflux transporter at intestinal epithelium, BBB, and kidney. P-gp inhibitors ↑ drug absorption/brain penetration: ritonavir, verapamil, amiodarone. P-gp inducers ↓ drug levels: rifampin, St. John's Wort. Digoxin and dabigatran are P-gp substrates

cards.front: Warfarin + vitamin K foods: clinical management
cards.back: Vitamin K antagonizes warfarin effect. Patients should maintain CONSISTENT (not zero) vitamin K intake. Sudden large intake of leafy greens (spinach, kale, broccoli) ↓ INR. Change in diet warrants INR recheck. Avoid drastic dietary changes

cards.front: Benzodiazepines + CNS depressants (FDA black box warning)
cards.back: BZDs + opioids → respiratory depression, coma, death. FDA requires black box warning. Other CNS depressants: alcohol, barbiturates, muscle relaxants, antihistamines, gabapentinoids. If combination unavoidable, use lowest doses and monitor closely

Probenecid inhibits renal tubular secretion (OAT transporter) → ↑ penicillin levels (used therapeutically to prolong effect), ↑ methotrexate (toxic), ↑ indomethacin. Also blocks uric acid reabsorption → used for gout

Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase → ↓ metabolism of azathioprine/6-MP → ↑↑ myelosuppression (pancytopenia, infection risk). If combination needed, reduce azathioprine dose by 75%. Monitor CBC closely

cards.front: Fluoride and calcium: interaction relevance to dental health
cards.back: Calcium (dairy, antacids) binds fluoride → ↓ fluoride absorption when taken together. Toothpaste/supplements: avoid immediately before/after calcium. Clinically relevant for systemic fluoride supplementation in children

Tramadol, meperidine, and fentanyl have serotonergic activity → risk of serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs, MAOIs, linezolid, triptans. Tramadol + SSRI: particularly high risk. Meperidine + MAOI: contraindicated (serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis)

Dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor) is a P-gp substrate but NOT metabolized by CYP450. P-gp inducers (rifampin) ↓ dabigatran → ↑ thrombosis risk. P-gp inhibitors (dronedarone, verapamil) ↑ dabigatran → ↑ bleeding risk. Renal dose adjustment also required

cards.front: Enzyme induction vs inhibition: time course
cards.back: Inhibition: rapid onset (within hours — competition for enzyme). Effect dissipates when inhibitor cleared. Induction: slow onset (days to weeks — requires new enzyme synthesis). Effect dissipates slowly after inducer stopped

Absolute contraindication. Bupropion inhibits dopamine/NE reuptake; MAOIs prevent catecholamine breakdown → hypertensive crisis, seizures. 14-day washout required when switching between MAOIs and bupropion (in either direction)

Carbamazepine induces its own metabolism (CYP3A4 autoinduction) — over 3–5 weeks, its own clearance increases and plasma levels drop. Dose may need adjustment 4–6 weeks after initiation. Also induces metabolism of phenytoin, valproate, warfarin, OCs

Valproate inhibits glucuronidation of lamotrigine → ↑ lamotrigine levels (↑ toxicity risk: Stevens-Johnson syndrome, CNS). Halve lamotrigine starting dose and titrate slowly when adding to valproate. Opposite: enzyme inducers ↓ lamotrigine

cards.front: Drug interaction with Vitamin K-rich foods (complete counseling point)
cards.back: INR monitoring is essential when patient changes diet. Consistent intake of vitamin K is key — not elimination. Vitamin K in leafy greens reduces warfarin effect (vitamin K competes for same carboxylation pathway). Document usual diet at INR clinic
Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) + β-blockers → additive AV node depression → bradycardia, heart block. Combination generally avoided; if used, monitor HR and ECG closely

Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor used as a pharmacokinetic 'booster' at subtherapeutic doses (e.g., lopinavir/ritonavir) to ↑ co-drug levels. Also inhibits CYP2D6, 2C9. Many drug interactions with co-medications

cards.front: Macrolide + statins + QT interval: combined risk
cards.back: Macrolides (esp. clarithromycin, erythromycin) inhibit CYP3A4 → ↑ simvastatin/atorvastatin → myopathy risk. Additionally macrolides themselves prolong QT. Double concern: myopathy + arrhythmia. Azithromycin: less CYP inhibition but more QT

Rifampin dramatically ↓ OC efficacy (↑ CYP3A4) → contraceptive failure. This interaction has led to unintended pregnancies in TB patients. Use additional/alternative contraception throughout rifampin course and for 1 month after stopping

cards.front: ACE inhibitor + aliskiren (direct renin inhibitor): risk
cards.back: Double renin-angiotensin blockade → ↑ hypotension, hyperkalemia, renal failure. Combination is contraindicated in patients with diabetes or CKD (per FDA). No significant benefit over single-agent therapy

Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 → ↑ theophylline by up to 30–50% → theophylline toxicity (narrow TI drug): seizures, arrhythmias, nausea. Enoxacin is the most potent inhibitor. Levofloxacin and moxifloxacin have minimal CYP1A2 inhibition

Linezolid is a weak, reversible MAOI → interactions with: SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome), dopaminergic agents, tyramine-rich foods, sympathomimetics. Duration of concern: same duration as linezolid therapy (usually 2 weeks). Monitor for serotonin syndrome

Low-dose MTX (rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis) inhibits dihydrofolate reductase → folate deficiency → mucositis, hepatotoxicity. Supplemental folic acid 1 mg/day (on non-MTX days) reduces toxicity without reducing anti-inflammatory efficacy
